content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
Jonathan Mintzer 
The Sutton Law Firm 
150 Post Street, Suite 405 
San Francisco, CA 9-l I 08 
Re: 
Your Request for Ad\·ic:c 
Our File No. A.•14-123 
Dear l'vlr. Mintzer: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322·0886 
August 8. 20 I -i 
This lencr responds lo your request for ad\ ice rcgan.ling the campaign pnn-ir.,ion'> of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"'). 
1 
QUESTION 
Docs Section 84223. requiring the muintcnam:e of a list of top IO conlrihutor., by 
committees that raise over S 1.000,000 and arc "primaril) formed to support or oppose a stale 
camlidatc ... apply lo a committee set up by a state candidate for hir., or her 0\\'n election or 
reelection effort'? 
CONCLUSION 
No, Section 84223. requiring committees primaril) formed to support or oppme a state 
candidate that raise over S 1.000.000 to submit a lir.,t to the FPPC of their top 10 contributor'>. 
doer., not apply lo a committee controlled by a state candidate for hir., or her own election or 
reelection. 
FACTS 
Senate Bill 27 \\'as pasr.,ed by the Legislature and signed h) the Governor on l\1ay 1-l. 
201-4.. The bill contains an urgenc) clause and became effecti\'c Jul) I. 201-l. The legislation 
describes when a multipurpose organization that is making political expenditure" in California 
qualifies as a committee under the Act and must file campaign reports disclo¶ing donors. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Gmwnmcnt Code Scctum¡ 81000 through 91014. All tJtul11r 
reference, an  10th.: Gmernmenl Code. unless otherwise indica1ed. l he rcgulJllnns uf the FJ1r Poh11cal Pr,1<:t1cc, 
Comntis,iun arc conlaincd in Sccllons 18110 through 1 X997 of Tulc 2 ol the Califurn1a Cmlc of Regulation,. All 
regulator) references arc 10 Title 2. Dt\'i,inn 6 of the California Cnde of RcgulJllon. unlc¡, other\\ hC 1nd11:atcd. 


File No. A-14-123 
Page No. 2 
The legislation also includes top contributor disclosure lists. State ballot measure 
committees and committees that are primarily formed to make independent expenditures 
supporting or opposing state candidates that have raised $1,000,000 or more are required to 
provide updated top 10 contributor lists to the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") for 
posting online. In addition, the bill requires the FPPC to compile and display on its website a list 
of the top lO contributors supporting and opposing each state ballot measure. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 84223 of the Act, added by SB 27, provides: 
"(a) A committee primarily formed to support or oppose a state ballot measure 
or state candidate that raises one million dollars ($1,000,000) or more for an 
election shall maintain an accurate list of the committee's top IO contributors, as 
specified by Commission regulations. A current list of the top IO contributors 
shall be provided to the Commission for disclosure on the Commission's Internet 
Website, as provided in subdivision (c)." 
The requirement to submit top IO contributor lists to the FPPC contained in Section 
84223(a) only applies to committees primarily formed to support or oppose a state ballot 
measure, and to committees primarily formed to make independent expenditures supporting or 
opposing a state candidate. The requirement docs not apply to a state candidate's own controlled 
committee for election to office. 
As you noted, this is made clear in the legislative findings and declarations for SB 27 
which provide: 
"(e) It is therefore the intent of the Legislature to strengthen the laws 
requiring the disclosure of contributions and expenditures in California elections 
by multipurpose organizations and to require committees that raise or spend one 
million dollars ($1,000,000) or more to support or oppose state ballot measure!, or 
make independent expenditures on behalf of a state candidate to disclose a list of 
their top IO contributors on the Internet Web site of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission." 
It is also clearly stated on new FPPC forms and instructions for the top IO contributor 
lists. In describing "Who Files," the instructions state "committees primarily formed to support 
or oppose a state candidate that raise $1,000,000 or more and have received contributions of 
$10,000 or more from a single source," and specify that "this does not include a committee 
controlled by a state candidate for his or her own election." 
The statute only requires top IO contributor lists from committees making independent 
expenditures on state candidates, because of the reasons you noted. First, the Act and regulations 


File No. A-14-123 
Page No. 3 
distinguish between a committee controlled by a state candidate for his or her own election and a 
committee primarily formed to support or oppose a state candidate through independent 
expenditures. Second, a committee controlled by a state candidate for his or her own election is 
subject to contribution limits under the Act, so there would not be many contributors giving large 
amounts in excess of$ I 0,000 for such candidates to list with the exception of candidates for the 
office of Governor. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HPW:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
'-,..; /11 
7)1.1 
rff()_ /, VVt( 
By: 
Hyla P. Wagner 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
